claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I can give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. To do this, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have a child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, but Alex doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I understand Alex doesn't currently live with you. 

Are you contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out a bit with things like clothes and bits and pieces for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility, I need to understand the level of your contributions.

Are you contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's upkeep? (For context, Child Benefit is currently around £25 per week for the eldest or only child, or about £102 per month.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, no, it's not that much. I contribute about £10 a week towards his things.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about that. I have one more important question to complete my assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also ask: who does Alex currently live with?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to be considered "responsible" for the child. According to the official rules, you're usually responsible for a child if either:
1. You live with them, OR
2. You're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them

In your situation:
- Alex doesn't live with you - he lives with his mother
- You contribute about £10 per week towards his upkeep
- However, Child Benefit for an eldest or only child is approximately £25 per week (about £102 per month)

Since your contributions (£10 per week) are less than the Child Benefit amount, you don't meet the threshold to be considered responsible for Alex under the rules.

**Important note:** Alex's mother would likely be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him, as she lives with him. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, so you may want to encourage her to apply if she hasn't already done so.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex comes to live with you, or if you increase your contributions to at least the Child Benefit amount - you could become eligible to claim.

reasonings: According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if either: (1) you live with them, or (2) you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. In Alex's case: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and you live in the UK, which meets the basic criteria. However, Alex does not live with you - he lives with his mother. You are contributing towards Alex's upkeep, but only about £10 per week. This is significantly less than the Child Benefit payment of approximately £25 per week for an eldest or only child. Therefore, you do not meet either of the two conditions for being considered 'responsible' for Alex under the Child Benefit rules. You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because you neither live with him nor contribute at least the equivalent amount of Child Benefit towards his upkeep.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (8 years old), who lives with his mother. While the claimant lives in the UK and makes some financial contributions (£10 per week), this does not meet the threshold required to qualify for Child Benefit when not living with the child. The rules require contributions of at least the same amount as Child Benefit (approximately £25 per week) to be considered responsible for a child who doesn't live with you. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

The rules require contributions of at least the same amount as Child Benefit (approximately £25 per week) to be considered responsible for a child who doesn't live with you.
